summary:
The Qianlong Emperor's reign (1735–1796 AD) represented the apogee of Qing imperial power and the zenith of traditional Chinese civilization — a 60-year tenure (the longest in Chinese imperial history, though he abdicated in 1796 to avoid surpassing his grandfather Kangxi) during which the empire achieved its greatest territorial extent, population growth, and cultural flowering. Qianlong completed the [[event:3299|Ten Great Campaigns]] that expanded Qing control into Central Asia, suppressing the [[event:3298|Dzungar Khanate]] and incorporating Xinjiang, Tibet, and other territories, while cultural projects like the Siku Quanshu (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) — a massive literary compilation of over 36,000 volumes — simultaneously celebrated and censored Chinese intellectual heritage through the [[event:3511|literary inquisition]].

The Qianlong reign also revealed structural weaknesses beneath imperial splendor. The emperor's favorite minister Heshen accumulated corrupt wealth estimated at over one billion taels of silver — perhaps the greatest official corruption in imperial history — while government efficiency declined under his influence. The [[event:3300|Macartney Mission]] of 1793, in which Britain sent its first embassy to China requesting expanded trade, was famously rebuffed by Qianlong ('We possess all things… we have no use for your country's manufactures') — a response that, while reflecting genuine self-sufficiency, also revealed imperial complacency about British industrial and military power. By the end of Qianlong's reign, the [[event:3245|White Lotus Rebellion]] had already begun, signaling the dynasty's gradual decline.
